series.title: [v2] hw/riscv/riscv-iommu: Use standard EN_PRI bit for PRI

review.summary: The functional change is small and appears directionally correct, but it introduces a guest-visible behavior change with no compatibility handling for VMs or machine types that previously enabled PRI through the old temporary TC[32] bit.

stages.A: Patch intent is clear: switch PRI enablement from the temporary custom TC[32] bit to the standard EN_PRI bit.
stages.B: The code change itself is mechanically correct if RISCV_IOMMU_DC_TC_EN_PRI is the architectural replacement for the temporary bit.
stages.C: Compatibility is not addressed. Existing migrated or resumed guests that still carry TC[32] set will stop triggering PRI until software rewrites the register.
stages.D: No build or runtime validation was provided, and checkpatch/build status were not run.
stages.E: Recommendation: add migration/machine-type compatibility handling or explicitly scope this to a new compat boundary.

findings.title: Changing PRI enable bit without compat handling can break migrated or existing guests
findings.description: Before this patch, PRI was enabled when `ctx->tc` had the temporary custom bit `BIT_ULL(32)` set. After this patch, the same runtime state no longer enables PRI unless the guest rewrites `tc` to set `RISCV_IOMMU_DC_TC_EN_PRI`. If `ctx->tc` is preserved across migration, reset, or compat machine types, a VM created by an older QEMU can silently lose PRI behavior after migration or upgrade.
findings.suggestion: Preserve backward compatibility by translating the legacy TC[32] bit to EN_PRI during vmstate/load or register write handling, or gate the new interpretation behind a machine-version compat knob so older machine types continue to honor TC[32].
findings.confidence_reason: The risk depends on how `ctx->tc` is serialized and whether compat machine types exist for this device, but QEMU device state is commonly migrated verbatim, so changing the meaning of a guest-visible register bit is a plausible compatibility regression.

disclaimer: LLM-generated draft. Not an authoritative review.
